title: Intro
text: Welcome to Science Radar, your weekly digest of the most surprising, frontier-pushing, and cross-disciplinary discoveries from across all of science. This week we've found papers that challenge long-held assumptions, open doors to entirely new research directions, and connect ideas across fields in ways nobody expected. Let's dive in.

title: This Week's Highlights
text: And now, a quick tour through this week's other noteworthy papers.

title: Dynamic modulation of social gaze by sex and familiarity in marmoset dyads
text: Dynamic modulation of social gaze by sex and familiarity in marmoset dyads — eLife. Social communication relies on the ability to perceive and interpret the direction of others’ attention, and is commonly conveyed through head orientation and gaze direction in humans and nonhuman primates. However, traditional social gaze experiments in nonhuman primates require restraining head mo

title: Transforming a fragile protein helix into an ultrastable scaffold via a hierarchical AI and chemistry framework
text: Transforming a fragile protein helix into an ultrastable scaffold via a hierarchical AI and chemistry framework — eLife. The rational design of proteins that maintain structural integrity under concurrent thermal, mechanical, and chemical stress remains a challenge in molecular engineering. We present a hierarchical framework that transforms an α-helical domain into an ultrastable scaffold by integrating AI-guided des

title: Extracellular vesicle-mediated release of bis(monoacylglycerol)phosphate is regulated by LRRK2 and glucocerebrosidase activity
text: Extracellular vesicle-mediated release of bis(monoacylglycerol)phosphate is regulated by LRRK2 and glucocerebrosidase activity — eLife. The endolysosomal phospholipid bis(monoacylglycerol)phosphate (BMP) is aberrantly elevated in urine from Parkinson’s patients carrying mutations in leucine-rich repeat kinase 2 (LRRK2) and glucocerebrosidase (GCase). Because BMP resides on, and regulates biogenesis of, endolysosomal intralumenal mem

title: Peptidoglycan recycling is critical for cell division, cell wall integrity, and β-lactam resistance in Caulobacter crescentus
text: Peptidoglycan recycling is critical for cell division, cell wall integrity, and β-lactam resistance in Caulobacter crescentus — eLife. Most bacteria possess a peptidoglycan (PG) sacculus, which is continuously remodeled during cell growth and division. The turnover products generated in this process are typically imported into the cell and reused for PG biosynthesis. While the underlying pathways have been studied intensively in ga

text:

title: Outro
text: That's Science Radar for this week. Every one of these papers is a reminder that the most interesting science often lives at the edges — where fields collide, where assumptions break, and where the next big question is just starting to take shape. Until next week, keep questioning everything.
